subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 60 minutes × 4 periods = 240 minutes
Total: 4 periods · 4h 0min

periods.activity_title: Entering the Poem: Before-Reading and Shared Poem Reading

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Before-reading: helping memory, word association, ragged meaning
Arrange poem events in correct order

phases.description: Before-reading (p.70): students write two feeling-words about a time they helped someone, then work in pairs to circle and underline words for elderly vs. school student. Teacher introduces the word 'ragged' with the MCQ context from the opening lines.

Teacher reads the poem aloud (p.70–72) with expression, then students re-read silently. Teacher highlights the AABB couplet structure and points to the simile 'like a flock of sheep' and the repetition of 'somebody's mother' as named in the poem appreciation summary.

Let us discuss: students arrange the eight poem events in correct sequence (p.73) individually, then compare with a partner. Class reviews the answer together, with teacher tracing the narrative arc from the woman's wait to her closing prayer.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem's AABB rhyme scheme gives it a ballad-like rhythm — read it at a steady pace so students hear the couplets. Students sometimes confuse 'the gayest laddie' with the general group of boys; clarify that only one boy pauses, as the appreciation summary notes. The fill-in task on theme, tone, and structure (task index 2) will be covered in the next period.

periods.activity_title: Poetic Devices, Imagery, and Extract-Level Analysis

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Fill blanks: theme, tone, structure, rhyme scheme
Identify simile, alliteration, repetition in the poem
Reason for repetition of 'somebody's mother'; analogy

homework.task_brief: Let us think and reflect (p.74): Extract 1: MCQ, reason, fact-opinion, ECR

phases.description: Teacher leads the fill-in-the-blank task on theme, tone, and rhyme scheme (Let us discuss II, p.73). Students answer individually, then teacher confirms answers — central theme is kindness and empathy, tone is sympathetic and hopeful, rhyme scheme AABB.

Poetic devices (Let us discuss III, p.73): teacher elicits one example of simile, alliteration, and repetition from students before confirming the textbook examples. Imagery task (Let us discuss IV, p.73) follows — students complete two sentences about the bustling street and wintry weather imagery.

Repetition and analogy tasks (Let us discuss V–VI, p.74): students complete 'The poet repeats the phrase Somebody's mother because…' and the analogy 'The old woman: vulnerable and neglected :: The young boy: ___'. Pairs share answers; teacher highlights the poem's moral pivot as described in the appreciation summary.
Homework set: Extract 1 questions (p.74). Teacher briefly reads the extract aloud so students have the lines fresh.

periods.teacher_notes: The appreciation summary identifies alliteration ('firm and strong') specifically — use this to model how to locate alliteration before students attempt independently. Imagery task 4 and the analogy task (task indices 4 and 5) work well together as they both hinge on contrasts between the woman and the boy.

periods.activity_title: Word-Search Antonyms, Matching Poem Words, and Hand Idioms

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Match poem words to underlined phrases

homework.task_brief: Let us learn (p.76): Word-search for antonyms of poem words

phases.description: Matching task (Let us learn, p.76): teacher reads the six words from the poem — hastened, heeded, stir, lend, piled, offered — and students match each to the underlined phrase in Column 2. Answers discussed briefly with attention to how each word is used in the poem's context.

'Hand' idioms (p.77): teacher introduces the six idioms with two example sentences, then students match idioms to meanings individually. Pairs compare and the class confirms. Teacher notes that 'lend a hand' appears in the poem itself — a direct link to the boy's act.

Homework: word-search antonyms task (p.76) for firm, meek, merry, kind, anxious, bent. Teacher also points out the analogy fill-in task (task index 3) as self-study; it can be attempted after the antonyms work.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem uses 'lend a helping hand' directly — students will likely spot this, making the idiom task feel grounded rather than abstract. The analogy fill-in (task index 3, p.77) is flagged as self-study since the period's task budget is full after the matching tasks.

periods.activity_title: Listening to a Hero, Narrating Kindness, and Writing a Diary Entry

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Fill blanks while listening to Rohan's news broadcast
Pair narration: incident where you helped someone
Write diary entry about helping someone in need

phases.description: Teacher reads or plays the listening transcript (p.99) about Rohan Ramchandra Bahir saving a woman from the Domri River. Students complete the five fill-in-the-blank sentences from Let us listen (p.78) on the second listening. Answers checked as a class.

Speaking (Let us speak, p.79): students work in pairs to narrate an incident where they helped someone, using cue cards (at school, at the market, in the neighbourhood, in the playground) and the sentence prompts. Each partner takes a turn. Teacher circulates and notes positive examples.

Writing (Let us write, p.80): teacher briefly recaps the diary entry format — date, salutation, narrative with feelings. Students draft their diary entries including the day's description, whom they helped, how, the person's reaction, and their own feelings. Teacher gives formative feedback during drafting.
Two students share a sentence or two from their diary. Teacher previews Section C (Verghese Kurien) for the next period, bridging the theme of service across all three sections.

periods.teacher_notes: The listening task index 0 (picture-sequencing) requires the printed textbook pictures; if unavailable, substitute with the fill-in task (index 1) already selected. The poem's closing prayer — 'God be kind to the noble boy, Who is somebody's son' — can be read aloud before the writing task to set the reflective tone.

items.source_lo: Student analyses a narrative poem's structure, devices, and theme using literary reading and comprehension discussion as the vehicle.
items.source_context: AABB couplets, simile, alliteration, repetition of 'somebody's mother', kindness and empathy theme.
items.item_stem:
Read the following statements about the poem 'Somebody's Mother'. Identify which statements are TRUE and which are FALSE.

1. The poem follows an AABB rhyme scheme in which every two consecutive lines rhyme with each other.
2. The line 'Came the boys like a flock of sheep' is an example of alliteration.
3. The poet repeats the phrase 'somebody's mother' to emphasise the shared humanity of every elderly person and to explain the boy's act of empathy.
4. The tone of the poem shifts from sympathetic and helpless to hopeful and warm as the boy steps forward to help the old woman.

options.text: The poem follows an AABB rhyme scheme in which every two consecutive lines rhyme with each other.
The line 'Came the boys like a flock of sheep' is an example of alliteration.
The poet repeats the phrase 'somebody's mother' to emphasise the shared humanity of every elderly person and to explain the boy's act of empathy.
The tone of the poem shifts from sympathetic and helpless to hopeful and warm as the boy steps forward to help the old woman.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. TRUE — The poem is written in couplets following an AABB rhyme scheme throughout. 2. FALSE — 'Came the boys like a flock of sheep' is a simile, not alliteration; an example of alliteration in the poem is 'firm and strong'. 3. TRUE — The repetition of 'somebody's mother' is the poem's moral pivot, reminding the reader that every elderly person is someone's loved one, which motivates the boy's compassion. 4. TRUE — The poem opens with the woman's helplessness and the crowd's indifference, then moves to hope and warmth when the boy helps her.

items.source_lo: Student listens to a news broadcast and retrieves sequential and factual details using listen-and-respond as the vehicle.
items.source_context: News broadcast about Rohan saving a woman from the Domri River; Pradhan Mantri Rashtriya Bal Puraskar 2023.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to a special news broadcast. As you listen, complete each sentence below using one to three exact words from the broadcast.

1. The woman Rohan saved had fallen into the ________ River while washing clothes.
2. Rohan leapt into the river without any ________, putting his own life at risk.
3. The onlookers who witnessed the rescue were in awe of the young boy's ________.
4. Rohan was honoured at Vigyan Bhawan with the ________ 2023 by the President of India.
5. Rohan's act proved that when it comes to saving lives, age is ________.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. Domri 2. moment's hesitation 3. bravery 4. Pradhan Mantri Rashtriya Bal Puraskar 5. no barrier

items.source_lo: Student narrates a personal experience of helping someone using structured pair narration as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Pair narration of a helping incident using cue cards: school, market, neighbourhood, playground.
items.item_stem: In 'Somebody's Mother', the boy helps the old woman without being asked, inspired by thinking of his own mother. Work in pairs. Take turns to narrate a real or imagined incident where you helped someone in need. Use ONE of the settings from the cue cards: at school / at the market / in the neighbourhood / in the playground. Your narration should include: (1) where and when it happened, (2) who needed help and what the situation was, (3) what you did, and (4) how both you and the other person felt afterwards.

expected_elements: Identifies the setting and situation clearly using the chosen cue card.
Describes the helping action with specific details.
Expresses the feelings of both the helper and the person helped.
Narrates in a logical sequence with appropriate past-tense language.

items.source_lo: Student writes a reflective diary entry about a personal experience using guided writing as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Diary entry about helping someone in need: situation, feelings during and after, motivation.
items.item_stem: The boy in 'Somebody's Mother' felt 'his young heart happy and well content' after helping the old woman cross the street. Write a diary entry from the perspective of the boy, describing the events of that day. Include: (1) a description of the old woman and the situation at the crossing, (2) what made you decide to help her when the others did not, (3) how you felt while guiding her and after returning to your friends, and (4) what you hope will happen for your own mother someday. Begin your entry with a suitable date and greeting, such as: 'Dear Diary, Today was a day I will not forget…'

expected_elements: Uses correct diary format with date, greeting, and first-person voice.
Describes the old woman's situation and the decision to help using details from the poem.
Reflects on feelings during and after the helping act with genuine emotion.
Connects the act to care for one's own mother, echoing the poem's theme.

items.source_lo: Student builds vocabulary through word-matching and idiom identification using a poem's language as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Poem words hastened, heeded, stir, lend, offered; idioms with 'hand'; antonyms of firm, meek, merry.
items.item_stem: Match each word from the poem 'Somebody's Mother' in Column A with the phrase in Column B that is closest in meaning.
items.visual_stimulus:
Column A | Column B
hastened | (i) paid careful attention to
heeded | (ii) moved quickly without stopping
stir | (iii) came forward with willingness to give
offered | (iv) give support or assistance
lend | (v) move slightly from a resting position
